title: The Open-Weight Awakening: LLaMA, Mistral, and the Models You Can Own
subtitle: How a leaked file in 2023 broke the monopoly and put frontier AI on your own laptop
theme: When the weights are free, intelligence stops being a service you rent and becomes a tool you own.
learningObjectives: Explain what 'open weights' actually means, and how it differs from open source and from closed APIs
Tell the story of LLaMA's release/leak and why it triggered an open-model Cambrian explosion
Map the key open-weight players, Meta's LLaMA, Mistral/Mixtral, Qwen, DeepSeek, and others, and what each contributed
Understand Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) and why it made open models efficient and competitive
Reason about the open-vs-closed debate on the real axes: control, cost, privacy, customization, safety, and licensing
Make a sound build decision: when an enterprise should run open weights locally versus call a closed API
storyHook: In February 2023, just ten weeks after ChatGPT lit the world on fire, Meta did something the other giants would not: it handed out the weights to a genuinely capable large language model, LLaMA, to approved researchers. Within days, a single anonymous post on 4chan leaked the entire model to the public internet. Meta's lawyers scrambled; the files spread faster than anyone could stop. And then something extraordinary happened. Thousands of developers, hobbyists, and startups grabbed the weights and, in a matter of weeks, built a thunderstorm of innovation that the locked-down labs could not match. The genie wasn't just out of the bottle, now anyone could keep one at home.

slides.title: Two Worlds: Rent vs. Own
slides.body:
## The fork in the road

After the ChatGPT moment, the AI world split into two philosophies, and the split still defines the market in 2026.

**World 1, Closed / API models.** OpenAI's GPT-4, Anthropic's Claude, Google's Gemini. You never see the model. It lives in the company's data center. You send text in, you get text out, you pay per token. You're *renting* intelligence. It's polished, powerful, and effortless, but you don't own it, can't inspect it, can't run it offline, and your data leaves your walls.

**World 2, Open-weight models.** LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, and a thousand descendants. The company *publishes the actual model weights*, the billions of trained numbers, for you to download. You can run them on your own hardware, modify them, fine-tune them, embed them in a product, and never send a byte to anyone else. You *own* a copy of the intelligence.

## Why this is a big deal

Imagine if, in the early days of electricity, you could only buy power from one of three utility companies, who metered every watt and could cut you off or change the price at will. Now imagine someone gave away the blueprint for a generator. Suddenly you could make your own power, in your basement, on your terms. That's what open weights did to AI.

> Closed models made AI *easy*. Open weights made AI *yours*. Today's enterprise AI strategy is largely a question of which one, and when, to use for which job.

Today we tell the story of how 'own it yourself' became possible, who made it happen, and how to decide between renting and owning.
slides.speakerNotes: Open with the rent-vs-own framing, it's the spine of the whole day and a decision your professional learners will actually make. The electricity/generator analogy lands hard, lean on it. Ask the room: 'How many of you have used ChatGPT or Claude? Now, how many knew you could download a comparably-capable model and run it on a laptop, for free, today?' The gap between those two numbers is why this lesson matters.
slides.marketConnection: In June 2026, nearly every serious enterprise runs a *hybrid* strategy, closed APIs for the hardest frontier tasks, open weights for privacy-sensitive, high-volume, or customized workloads, making 'rent vs. own, per use case' a core architectural decision.

slides.title: What 'Open Weights' Actually Means (and Doesn't)
slides.body:
## Three different kinds of 'open'

This trips up even experienced people, so let's be precise. There's a spectrum:

1. **Closed (API-only):** You get *no* model, just an endpoint. Example: GPT-4, Claude, Gemini.
2. **Open weights:** You get the *trained model file* (the weights), free to download and run. But you usually do NOT get the training data, the full training code, or sometimes even full freedom in the license. Example: most LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen releases. **This is the dominant 'open' category, and the one that matters most commercially.**
3. **Fully open source:** Weights *plus* training data, training code, and a permissive license, everything needed to reproduce the model from scratch. Rarer (e.g., efforts like OLMo, Pythia). The gold standard for reproducibility and research.

## Why the distinction matters

People loosely call open-weight models 'open source', but it's often not true in the strict sense. With open *weights*:

- You CAN: run it offline, on your own servers; fine-tune it on your data; inspect its behavior; embed it in products; avoid per-call fees.
- You usually CANNOT: see what data it was trained on, fully reproduce it, or (depending on license) use it without restrictions.

## The weights ARE the model

Remember from earlier days: a trained model is just an enormous list of numbers, the weights, that encode everything it learned. Hand someone those numbers plus the (public) architecture, and they have the complete, runnable mind. That's why a single leaked file in 2023 was so explosive: the weights are the whole ballgame.

```
Architecture (public, e.g. a Transformer)
        +
Weights  (the trained numbers)          =   a runnable model
        +
(optional) training data & code          =   fully reproducible model
```

> 'Open weights' means you own a runnable copy of the intelligence. It does NOT necessarily mean you know how it was made, or that you can do absolutely anything you want with it, the license still matters (we'll get there).
slides.speakerNotes: Precision here prevents a lot of downstream confusion. Draw the three-tier spectrum on the board. The key correction to install: 'open weights' is not the same as 'open source', and most famous 'open' models are open-*weight*, not fully open-source. Use the 'weights = the whole runnable mind' point to explain why a leak was catastrophic. Ask: 'If you have the weights but not the training data, what can't you do?'
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, this distinction is legally and strategically critical: enterprises scrutinize whether a model is truly open-source or merely open-weight-with-a-restrictive-license, because it determines what they can legally build and ship.

slides.title: The Leak Heard Around the World
slides.body:
## February 2023: Meta opens a door

The context: ChatGPT had just proven the world wanted this. OpenAI and Google guarded their best models tightly. Meta, behind in the consumer chatbot race but deep in AI research, took a different bet. On February 24, 2023, it announced **LLaMA** (Large Language Model Meta AI), a family of genuinely strong models (7B to 65B parameters). The pitch: smaller, efficient models that performed shockingly well, the 13B version rivaled the original 175B GPT-3. Meta released the weights, but only to *approved researchers* who applied for access.

That gate lasted about a week.

## The leak

On March 3, 2023, someone posted a downloadable torrent of the full LLaMA weights on 4chan. The files were suddenly *everywhere*, GitHub, torrents, Discord servers. Meta's legal team sent takedowns; it was hopeless. You cannot un-ring a bell on the internet. The weights to a near-frontier model were now in the hands of anyone who wanted them.

## What happened next was the real story

Meta might have panicked. But the global developer community treated the leak like rocket fuel. In *weeks*:

- **llama.cpp** appeared, letting LLaMA run on an ordinary laptop CPU, even a Raspberry Pi, no data center required.
- **Alpaca** (Stanford): researchers fine-tuned LLaMA into an instruction-follower for about *600 dollars*, approximating ChatGPT-like behavior on a shoestring.
- **Vicuna, Koala, and dozens more** followed within a month, each cheaply fine-tuned for chat.
- Quantization tricks shrank the models to run on consumer GPUs and even phones.

## The leaked Google memo

That May, an internal Google document leaked, titled **'We Have No Moat, And Neither Does OpenAI.'** Its stunned argument: the open-source community was innovating *faster* than the giant labs, building on LLaMA, because thousands of minds iterating in the open beat any single company. It became the defining text of the era.

> The lesson: capability, once the weights are free, *compounds in the commons.* A leak meant to be contained instead seeded an ecosystem the closed labs could not out-run.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the dramatic heart of the day, tell it like a thriller: the gated release, the 4chan torrent, the futile takedowns, then the explosion of creativity. The Alpaca '$600 to approximate ChatGPT' fact reliably gets gasps. The 'We Have No Moat' memo is the philosophical climax, read its title aloud. Ask: 'Why would thousands of independent developers out-innovate a billion-dollar lab?' (Parallelism, no bureaucracy, sharing.)
slides.marketConnection: The 'No Moat' thesis has only partly held by 2026, frontier closed models retain an edge on the hardest tasks, but open models dominate huge swathes of practical deployment, vindicating the core claim that an open ecosystem moves astonishingly fast.

slides.title: From Leak to Strategy: Meta Goes All-In
slides.body:
## Turning an accident into a doctrine

Here's the plot twist. Meta could have fought the open-weight tide. Instead, it *embraced* it as deliberate strategy. The reasoning was shrewd: if Meta can't win the closed-API race against OpenAI and Google, it can *commoditize* the layer they're trying to monopolize. Make powerful models free, and you erode your rivals' pricing power while building the entire ecosystem on *your* foundation. (There's an old Silicon Valley move here: 'commoditize your complement.')

## The LLaMA cadence

- **LLaMA 2 (July 2023):** officially released with a license permitting *commercial* use (with some conditions). This was the watershed, now companies could legally *build products* on open weights. It came in 7B / 13B / 70B sizes, with chat-tuned versions. Adoption exploded.
- **LLaMA 3 / 3.1 (2024):** a major leap, including a massive 405B model that, for the first time, put an *open* model squarely in the same conversation as the best closed frontier models on many benchmarks. Strong 8B and 70B versions made high quality accessible at every scale.
- **Continued releases (into 2025-2026):** larger context windows, multimodality, and ever-stronger small models, with Meta positioning LLaMA as the default open foundation worldwide.

## Why enterprises cheered

LLaMA 2's commercial license flipped a switch. Suddenly a bank, a hospital, or a startup could take a top-tier model, run it *inside their own firewall*, fine-tune it on proprietary data, and ship it, no data leaving the building, no per-token meter, no vendor lock-in.

> Meta turned a leak into a movement, and a movement into a moat-of-a-different-kind: own the foundation everyone else builds on. By 2026, 'LLaMA-based' is to open AI what 'Linux-based' is to servers.
slides.speakerNotes: Frame Meta's pivot as strategic genius, 'commoditize your complement', it reframes open-sourcing from charity to chess. The LLaMA 2 commercial license is the single most important business beat: it's what made open weights *enterprise-legal*. Use the Linux analogy for the closer. Ask: 'Why would a giant company give away something this valuable? What does Meta actually gain?'
slides.marketConnection: As of June 2026, the LLaMA family is the most widely deployed open-weight foundation in the world, the 'Linux of AI', underpinning countless enterprise systems precisely because it can run privately and be customized freely under a commercial-friendly license.

slides.title: Enter Mistral: Europe's Lean Powerhouse
slides.body:
## A few months old, instantly world-class

In 2023, a brand-new French startup, **Mistral AI**, founded by ex-DeepMind and ex-Meta researchers, did something audacious. With a tiny team and a fraction of Big Tech's resources, they released **Mistral 7B** (September 2023), a 7-billion-parameter model that *outperformed* LLaMA 2 13B, nearly double its size. The message was electric: clever engineering could beat brute scale. And they released it under the gold-standard **Apache 2.0** license, fully permissive, no strings.

They announced it with characteristic flair, just a magnet (torrent) link on Twitter, no marketing fluff. The open community loved it.

## Mixtral and the efficiency story

Then came **Mixtral 8x7B** (December 2023), which introduced many developers to a powerful idea: **Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)** (next slide). Mixtral matched or beat much larger dense models while being far cheaper to *run*, because it only activates a fraction of its parameters per token. Efficiency, not just size, became a headline feature.

## Why Mistral mattered

- **Proof that small teams could play:** you didn't need to be Google to ship a competitive model.
- **Truly permissive licensing:** Apache 2.0 on key models removed legal friction entirely.
- **A European, sovereign alternative:** crucial for governments and firms wary of depending on U.S. labs, 'AI sovereignty' became a real strategic theme.
- **Efficiency leadership:** Mistral kept pushing strong *small* models, perfect for running locally and cheaply.

> Mistral proved two things at once: that engineering ingenuity can rival raw scale, and that the open-weight movement was now a *global* phenomenon, not a one-company accident.
slides.speakerNotes: Mistral is the 'David vs. Goliath' beat, a months-old startup beating models twice its size. The 'just a magnet link on Twitter' detail captures the culture, contrast it with corporate launch theater. Flag Apache 2.0 as a genuine business advantage over more restrictive licenses. Introduce MoE here as a teaser for the next slide. Ask: 'What does it tell you that a tiny startup could match Big Tech's models?'
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, Mistral is a leading European AI champion and a symbol of 'AI sovereignty'; its permissively licensed, efficient models are enterprise favorites where legal cleanliness and on-prem cost-efficiency matter most, especially across the EU.

slides.title: Mixture-of-Experts: Big Brain, Small Bill
slides.body:
## The trick that made open models efficient

A traditional ('dense') model uses *all* its parameters to process *every* token. A 70B dense model does 70B-worth of computation for each word, expensive and slow.

**Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)** changes the deal. Instead of one giant brain, the model contains many smaller specialist sub-networks called **experts**, plus a small **router** that, for each token, picks just a few experts to consult.

```
            token
              |
        +-----------+
        |  ROUTER   |  picks (e.g.) 2 of 8 experts
        +-----------+
         /    |    \  
   [E1] [E2] [E3] ... [E8]    <- only 2 light up per token
         \    |    /
          combine -> output
```

So a Mixtral '8x7B' has 8 experts but activates only ~2 per token. It holds the *knowledge* of a large model (many total parameters) while paying the *compute* of a small one (few active parameters per token).

## The analogy

Think of a hospital. A patient with a heart issue doesn't get examined by *every* doctor in the building, the front desk (router) sends them to the cardiologist and maybe one other specialist. You get expert care without mobilizing the entire staff for every patient. MoE is that triage, applied to neural networks.

## Why it mattered for open models

- **Big capability, small running cost:** crucial when *you* are paying for the GPUs (open, self-hosted), not a cloud giant absorbing the cost.
- **Faster inference:** fewer active parameters means quicker responses.
- **Democratization:** strong models you can actually afford to run.

## The catch

MoE models still need enough *memory* to hold ALL experts (all those total parameters sit in RAM/VRAM), even though only a few compute per token. So they're cheaper to *run* but still hungry for *memory*. There's no free lunch, just a better trade.

> MoE is the headline efficiency idea of the open era: get the knowledge of a giant while paying the compute bill of a dwarf. By 2026, nearly every frontier model, open and closed, uses some form of it.
slides.speakerNotes: MoE is the one piece of architecture in this day, keep it intuitive with the hospital-triage analogy and the router diagram. The crucial nuance professionals miss: cheaper *compute*, but still needs memory for *all* experts. That nuance affects real deployment decisions. Ask: 'Why does MoE matter MORE for someone self-hosting an open model than for a cloud API user?' (Because the self-hoster pays the compute directly.)
slides.marketConnection: By June 2026, MoE is essentially universal at the frontier, it's the standard way to scale knowledge without exploding inference cost, and understanding 'total vs. active parameters' is essential for sizing the hardware to self-host a model.

slides.title: The Cambrian Explosion: A World of Models
slides.body:
## Suddenly, models everywhere

Once LLaMA proved it could be done and Mistral proved small teams could compete, the floodgates opened. The result was a genuine **Cambrian explosion**, an eruption of diverse, capable open-weight models from around the globe. The key players to know:

### Meta , LLaMA family
The foundation everyone builds on. Widely fine-tuned, broadly licensed, the 'Linux of AI.'

### Mistral / Mixtral (France)
Lean, efficient, permissively licensed; MoE pioneers in the open space; the European standard-bearer.

### Qwen (Alibaba, China)
A powerhouse, and arguably the most *prolific* open-weight family by 2026. Strong across sizes, excellent multilingual (especially Chinese + English) and coding ability, frequently topping open leaderboards. A huge share of fine-tuned community models are Qwen-based.

### DeepSeek (China)
Shook the world in early 2025 with **DeepSeek-R1**, an open *reasoning* model rivaling top closed reasoning models, reportedly trained far more cheaply than expected. It detonated the assumption that frontier reasoning required closed labs and astronomical budgets, and rattled markets.

### Google , Gemma
Google's open-weight line (smaller siblings to closed Gemini), bringing big-lab quality into the open ecosystem.

### Others worth knowing
**Microsoft Phi** (tiny but mighty 'small language models' trained on high-quality data), **Falcon** (UAE), **Yi** (China), and fully-open efforts like **OLMo** (Allen Institute) for true reproducibility.

## The Hugging Face hub: the GitHub of models

All of this lives on **Hugging Face**, the central repository where hundreds of thousands of open models, datasets, and fine-tunes are shared, downloaded, and ranked on leaderboards. It became the beating heart of the open-AI commons, if open weights are the Linux of AI, Hugging Face is its GitHub.

> The lesson: open weights didn't produce *one* alternative to closed AI, they produced a teeming, global, fast-evolving *ecosystem*, with serious contributions from the US, Europe, China, and the Middle East.
slides.speakerNotes: Don't make this a dry roll-call, give each player a one-line identity learners will remember (LLaMA = foundation, Mistral = lean European, Qwen = prolific multilingual, DeepSeek = the cheap-reasoning shock, Gemma = Google's open line). The DeepSeek-R1 story deserves emphasis, it genuinely moved stock markets in early 2025. Point them to Hugging Face as the place to actually go try this. Ask: 'Notice how many of these are non-American. What does that mean for the AI race?'
slides.marketConnection: As of June 2026 the open ecosystem is fiercely multipolar, Chinese labs (Qwen, DeepSeek) are open-weight leaders, Europe has Mistral, and Hugging Face hosts hundreds of thousands of models; for enterprises this means abundant choice, but also new geopolitical and supply-chain considerations about which models to trust and deploy.

slides.title: Running Models Locally: AI on Your Own Machine
slides.body:
## From data center to desktop

One of the most empowering consequences of open weights: you can run a genuinely capable AI **on your own computer**, fully offline, completely private. The tooling that made this approachable:

- **llama.cpp** , the breakthrough engine that runs models efficiently on ordinary CPUs and consumer GPUs.
- **Ollama** , a dead-simple tool: `ollama run llama3` and you're chatting with a local model in seconds. It made local AI accessible to non-experts.
- **LM Studio, GPT4All, Jan** , friendly desktop apps with a ChatGPT-like UI, but the model runs on *your* hardware.
- **vLLM, TGI** , high-performance servers for businesses self-hosting models at scale.

## The magic ingredient: quantization

Full-precision weights are big (a 70B model can be ~140GB). **Quantization** compresses the numbers, e.g., from 16-bit down to 4-bit, shrinking the model dramatically (that 70B might drop to ~40GB) with only a small quality loss. This is what lets serious models fit on a gaming GPU or a MacBook.

```
7B model, 4-bit quantized  -> runs on a modern laptop
70B model, 4-bit quantized -> runs on a high-end workstation / one big GPU
```

## Why running locally is a superpower

- **Privacy:** your data NEVER leaves your machine, decisive for healthcare, legal, finance, defense.
- **No per-call cost:** run it a million times for the price of electricity.
- **Offline & reliable:** works on a plane, in a secure facility, with no internet, no rate limits, no vendor outage.
- **Full control & customization:** fine-tune, modify, embed, no one can change or revoke it.
- **No lock-in:** the model is yours, forever, exactly as downloaded.

## The trade-offs (be honest)

- The very best *frontier* quality usually still lives in big closed models.
- You manage the hardware, updates, and scaling yourself.
- Capable models still need decent hardware (RAM/VRAM is the usual bottleneck).

> The barrier between 'AI is a service in the cloud' and 'AI is software I run' has collapsed. For a growing share of tasks, you can simply *own* the intelligence and keep it in the building.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the most hands-on, empowering slide, if you can, demo `ollama run` live; watching a real model boot on a laptop is unforgettable. Make quantization concrete with the size numbers. Emphasize privacy as the killer enterprise driver (tie to your audience's regulated industries). Be honest about trade-offs so they make good decisions. Ask: 'For what kind of data would your organization NEVER want to call an external API?'
slides.marketConnection: By June 2026, local and on-prem open-model deployment is mainstream in regulated industries, tools like Ollama and vLLM are standard, quantized models run on everyday hardware, and 'keep sensitive AI inside the firewall' is a default requirement in healthcare, finance, legal, and government.

slides.title: The Open vs. Closed Debate, On the Real Axes
slides.body:
## Not 'which is better', but 'which for what'

The mature question is never 'open or closed?' in the abstract, it's *which one for this specific job?* Let's reason on the axes that actually matter.

| Axis | Closed (API) | Open weights |
|---|---|---|
| **Peak capability** | Usually leads on hardest frontier tasks | Close behind, leads on many practical tasks |
| **Ease of use** | Effortless, just an API call | You manage hosting/ops (or use a host) |
| **Cost at scale** | Per-token fees add up fast | Pay for hardware; cheap at high volume |
| **Privacy / data** | Data leaves your walls | Runs fully in your environment |
| **Customization** | Limited (some fine-tuning offered) | Total, fine-tune and modify freely |
| **Control / lock-in** | Vendor can change/deprecate/price-hike | You own the exact weights forever |
| **Offline** | No | Yes |
| **Maintenance burden** | Vendor handles it | You handle it |

## The safety argument (both sides are serious)

- **Pro-open:** transparency enables independent scrutiny, security research, and trust; no single company controls the technology; it democratizes access and prevents monopoly.
- **Pro-closed (safety concern):** once weights are released, *you cannot recall them*, and anyone can strip the safety training off an open model (it's been demonstrated, cheaply) to produce an uncensored version for misuse. Closed models keep a 'kill switch' and gate dangerous capabilities.

This tension, *openness enables both innovation and misuse*, is genuinely unresolved and now sits at the center of AI policy worldwide.

## The pragmatic synthesis

> Most serious organizations in 2026 don't pick a side, they go **hybrid**: closed frontier models for the hardest, lowest-volume, highest-stakes reasoning; open weights for privacy-sensitive, high-volume, cost-sensitive, customizable, or offline workloads. The skill is routing each task to the right tool.
slides.speakerNotes: Resist letting this become a tribal 'open good / closed bad' argument, model the mature, axis-by-axis reasoning. Walk the table column by column. Present BOTH safety arguments fairly, the 'you can't recall released weights, and safety training can be stripped' point is the strongest case for caution, and learners should hold it honestly alongside the pro-open case. Land on 'hybrid + routing' as the real-world answer. Ask: 'Name a task you'd send to a closed model, and one you'd keep on open weights. Why?'
slides.marketConnection: Hybrid is the default 2026 enterprise architecture; meanwhile the open-vs-closed safety debate is a live regulatory battleground (e.g., rules on releasing powerful model weights), making this not just a technical choice but a compliance and policy one.

slides.title: Licensing: Read the Fine Print Before You Ship
slides.body:
## 'Open' is not one thing, legally

Here's where excited builders get burned. A model can be downloadable for free and *still* carry real legal restrictions. Before you build a business on a model, you MUST read its license. The main flavors:

### Truly permissive , Apache 2.0 / MIT
Do almost anything, commercial use, modify, redistribute, with minimal conditions. Examples: Mistral's key models, many others. **The cleanest for business.**

### Open-but-conditional , custom 'community' licenses
The biggest gotcha. LLaMA's license, for instance, permits commercial use but adds conditions (e.g., historically an acceptable-use policy, and a clause requiring very large companies, above a user threshold, to seek a separate license). Not OSI-'open-source', but workable for most. **Read the specific terms.**

### Non-commercial / research-only
Some models (and especially many *fine-tunes*) are released for research only, you may NOT use them in a commercial product. Also beware: a model fine-tuned on data generated by a closed model (e.g., GPT-4 outputs) may inherit usage restrictions from *that* provider's terms.

## The traps to watch for

- **License inheritance:** a fine-tune is bound by the base model's license AND the training-data's terms. Stacking can quietly forbid commercial use.
- **'Open-washing':** marketing something as 'open' that has heavy restrictions. Verify, don't trust the headline.
- **Acceptable-use policies:** even permissive-ish licenses often forbid certain uses (illegal, harmful). These bind you.
- **Changing terms:** licenses can differ version to version of the same model family.

## The rule

> Free to download does NOT mean free to use however you like. For anything you'll ship, have someone actually read the license, and remember licenses stack: base model + fine-tune + training data. When in doubt, prefer Apache 2.0 / MIT for commercial peace of mind.
slides.speakerNotes: Unsexy but career-saving, professionals genuinely get this wrong and create legal exposure. The single most important concept: license inheritance/stacking (base + fine-tune + data terms). Use a concrete cautionary framing: 'You built a product on a model someone fine-tuned on GPT-4 outputs, congratulations, you may have just violated two licenses.' Ask: 'Whose job is it in your org to vet a model's license before launch?' (Often nobody's, that's the risk.)
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, model licensing is a standard part of enterprise procurement and legal review; 'open-washing' is a recognized issue, and many companies maintain approved-model lists vetted for license compliance before any production use.

slides.title: The Decision Framework: Should You Run Open Weights?
slides.body:
## A practical playbook for the build-vs-buy call

You're a team lead in June 2026. A project needs an LLM. Open weights (self-host) or closed API? Walk this decision tree.

### Lean toward OPEN WEIGHTS (self-host) when:
- **Data is sensitive/regulated** , medical records, legal docs, financial data, defense. It must not leave your environment.
- **Volume is very high** , at millions of calls, owning hardware crushes per-token API fees on cost.
- **You need deep customization** , fine-tuning on proprietary data, modifying behavior, specialized domains.
- **You need offline / air-gapped operation** , secure facilities, edge devices, no reliable internet.
- **You require stability/control** , can't risk a vendor deprecating, changing, or price-hiking the model under you.
- **The task is well-scoped** , a focused job a good mid-size open model handles easily (most real tasks!).

### Lean toward CLOSED API when:
- **You need absolute peak capability** , the hardest reasoning, where the frontier closed model still wins.
- **You want zero ops burden** , no GPUs to manage, fastest path to a prototype.
- **Volume is low/spiky** , pay-as-you-go beats buying idle hardware.
- **You move fast and iterate** , instant access to the newest, best models.

### The hybrid reality (what most actually do)
```
User request
     |
  [ Router ]
   /      \
simple/    hard/rare/
private/   highest-stakes
high-vol      |
   |       Closed frontier API
Open model
(self-hosted)
```
Route the bulk of traffic to a cheap, private, fast open model; escalate the rare hard cases to a premium closed model. Best of both: cost + privacy on the many, peak quality on the few.

> The 2026 professional doesn't ask 'open or closed?' They ask 'what does THIS task need?', and increasingly, they build systems that use both.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the slide your professional audience came for, the actionable decision logic. Walk both lists, then the hybrid router diagram (it ties back to MoE-style 'route to the right resource' thinking and previews agent architectures later). Make it personal: have a few learners describe a real project and crowd-source which way it should go. Ask: 'For YOUR current project, which side does the decision tree point to, and which factor decided it?'
slides.marketConnection: This exact build-vs-buy reasoning is a daily decision in 2026 engineering orgs; the hybrid 'route by task' architecture (often with a cheap open model as the default and a closed model as escalation) is now a standard, cost-and-privacy-optimized design pattern.

slides.title: The Big Picture: Why Open Weights Changed the Game
slides.body:
## The arc of the day

Let's tie it together.

- After ChatGPT, AI split into **rent (closed APIs)** and **own (open weights)**.
- **Open weights** means you get a runnable copy of the model, though usually not the training data, and the license still binds you.
- In Feb-Mar 2023, **LLaMA's release and leak** seeded an ecosystem; within weeks, llama.cpp and Alpaca proved capable AI could run cheaply, anywhere. Google's **'No Moat' memo** captured the shock.
- **Meta leaned in** (LLaMA 2's commercial license made open weights enterprise-legal; LLaMA 3 reached the frontier), **Mistral** proved small teams and clever engineering (and MoE) could compete, and a global **Cambrian explosion** followed, **Qwen, DeepSeek, Gemma, Phi**, and more, hosted on **Hugging Face**.
- **MoE** delivered big-model knowledge at small-model running cost. **Quantization + Ollama/llama.cpp** put real models on laptops, private and offline.
- The **open-vs-closed** choice comes down to real axes (capability, cost, privacy, control, licensing, safety), and the mature answer is **hybrid**: route each task to the right tool.

## Three ideas to carry forever

1. **When the weights are free, intelligence becomes infrastructure you own, not just a service you rent**, and that shifts power from a few labs to everyone.
2. **The open ecosystem compounds**: thousands of builders iterating on shared weights move at a speed no single lab can match (the 'No Moat' thesis), though the frontier closed edge persists.
3. **The skill is judgment, not loyalty**: don't be an 'open person' or a 'closed person', be someone who routes each task to the right model, with eyes open on cost, privacy, license, and safety.

## Why this matters for you in 2026

Your organization's AI strategy, its costs, its privacy posture, its independence from any one vendor, hinges on understanding the rent-vs-own landscape you now know. You can read a model card, vet a license, size the hardware, and make the build-vs-buy call. That's real power.
slides.speakerNotes: Synthesize calmly, redraw the rent-vs-own spine and the LLaMA-to-ecosystem timeline. Hammer the three carry-forever ideas, especially 'judgment, not loyalty', it inoculates against tribalism. Connect to their world: they can now make an actual strategic decision for their team. Close the three-day mini-arc (prompting -> alignment -> open ecosystem) and tease forward. Ask: 'After these three days, has your view of where AI power lives changed?'
slides.marketConnection: Fluency in the open ecosystem is, in 2026, a strategic necessity, it determines an organization's AI cost structure, data sovereignty, and vendor independence, which is exactly why this is core professional knowledge, not niche trivia.

slides.title: Quick Check: Did It Stick?
slides.body:
## Test yourself (answers below)

**1.** 'Open weights' most precisely means:
   a) You get the full training data and code to reproduce the model
   b) You get the trained model file to download and run, but often not the data/code, and the license still applies
   c) You can only access it via API
   d) It is always free for any commercial use

**2.** The 2023 LLaMA leak was so significant because:
   a) It revealed OpenAI's secrets
   b) The weights are the complete runnable model, so the community could instantly build on it, and did, explosively
   c) It crashed Meta's servers
   d) It was the first AI model ever

**3.** Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) lets a model:
   a) Use all parameters on every token for max quality
   b) Hold large total knowledge while activating only a few experts per token, cheaper compute, but still needs memory for all experts
   c) Eliminate the need for any GPU
   d) Run without any weights

**4.** Which factor most strongly pushes a project toward self-hosting open weights?
   a) You want the absolute peak frontier capability with zero ops work
   b) Highly sensitive/regulated data that must not leave your environment
   c) Very low, spiky request volume
   d) You want the newest model the instant it launches

**5.** A key licensing trap with open models is:
   a) They never have any license
   b) License inheritance/stacking, a fine-tune is bound by the base model's AND the training data's terms, which can forbid commercial use
   c) Apache 2.0 forbids all commercial use
   d) Licenses never change between versions

**6.** The mature 2026 stance on open vs. closed is:
   a) Closed is always better
   b) Open is always better
   c) Hybrid, route each task to the right tool based on capability, cost, privacy, control, and licensing
   d) Avoid LLMs entirely

---

### Answers
**1: b** , a runnable copy, usually without data/code, license still binds.
**2: b** , weights = the whole model, so the commons could build on it immediately.
**3: b** , big knowledge, few active experts per token; still memory-hungry.
**4: b** , data privacy/regulation is the strongest pull toward self-hosting.
**5: b** , licenses stack; verify base + fine-tune + data terms.
**6: c** , hybrid + task-routing is the professional answer.

## Discussion
Pick a real use case from your work. Using the decision framework, argue out loud whether it should run on open weights or a closed API, and name the ONE factor that decides it. Then state which specific model family you'd reach for and why.
slides.speakerNotes: Fast, energizing close. Spend the most time on Q4 (privacy as the decisive self-host factor) and Q5 (license stacking, the real-world trap). For the discussion, push for a *specific* model choice and a *single decisive factor*, that crispness is what good architects bring. Reward 'why are the wrong options wrong' reasoning. End by celebrating: 'You can now make a real AI build-vs-buy decision, that's a genuinely valuable skill.'
slides.marketConnection: Being able to argue a concrete open-vs-closed decision, with a named model and a decisive factor, is precisely the practical judgment 2026 employers and clients pay for.

keyTakeaways: AI split into two worlds, rent (closed APIs like GPT-4/Claude/Gemini) and own (open weights like LLaMA/Mistral/Qwen); 'open weights' means a runnable copy you can self-host, fine-tune, and keep private, though usually without training data and still bound by a license.

LLaMA's Feb-Mar 2023 release and leak seeded an open ecosystem that innovated stunningly fast (llama.cpp, Alpaca for ~$600, the 'No Moat' memo); Meta then made it strategy, and LLaMA 2's commercial license made open weights enterprise-legal.

A global Cambrian explosion followed, Mistral/Mixtral (lean, European, MoE, Apache 2.0), Qwen (prolific, multilingual), DeepSeek-R1 (cheap open reasoning that shook markets), Gemma, Phi, all hosted on Hugging Face, the 'GitHub of models'.

Mixture-of-Experts gives big-model knowledge at small-model compute cost by activating only a few experts per token (but still needs memory for all of them); with quantization and tools like Ollama, capable models now run privately on a laptop.

Open vs. closed is decided on real axes, capability, cost-at-scale, privacy, customization, control/lock-in, offline, and safety (released weights can't be recalled and safety training can be stripped); the mature answer is hybrid: route each task to the right tool.

Licensing is load-bearing: 'free to download' is not 'free to use'; watch for license inheritance/stacking (base + fine-tune + data), 'open-washing', and acceptable-use clauses, prefer Apache 2.0/MIT for commercial peace of mind.
marketSnapshot2026: By June 2026 the open-weight ecosystem is a thriving, multipolar pillar of the AI economy rather than an underdog. Open models trail closed frontier systems only on the very hardest tasks while leading on a huge share of practical, high-volume, and privacy-sensitive work, and the landscape is strikingly global: Meta's LLaMA is the 'Linux of AI' foundation, Europe's Mistral champions efficiency and 'AI sovereignty', and Chinese labs, Alibaba's Qwen and DeepSeek, are open-weight leaders (DeepSeek-R1's cheap open reasoning notably rattled markets in early 2025). Mixture-of-Experts is now near-universal at the frontier, and quantization plus tools like Ollama, vLLM, and Hugging Face have made local and on-prem deployment standard in regulated industries. Enterprises overwhelmingly run hybrid architectures, routing most traffic to cheap, private, self-hosted open models and escalating the hardest cases to closed APIs, while treating model licensing and the open-vs-closed safety debate (you can't recall released weights; safety filters can be stripped) as core compliance and policy matters.
homework: Hands-on + decision. (1) Install Ollama (or LM Studio) and run an open model locally, e.g. `ollama run llama3` or a small Qwen/Mistral model. Ask it three things you'd normally ask ChatGPT and write 5 sentences comparing the experience, quality, speed, and how it felt to run AI fully on your own machine with no internet. (2) Take a real project or idea from your work and write a one-page open-vs-closed recommendation using the Day 9 decision framework: name the decisive factor (privacy? cost? capability? control?), pick a specific model family, and check its license for any commercial restrictions. Bonus: look up one model on Hugging Face and read its model card and license.
nextDayTeaser: We've watched models learn to predict, to listen, and to be owned. But so far they only do *one* thing: read text and write text back. What if a model could also *see* an image, *hear* your voice, *watch* a video, and reason across all of them at once? Tomorrow we cross into the multimodal frontier, where AI grows new senses, and the line between language model and world model starts to blur.
